none: Chicago underflow plan, urban water damage study : environmental impact statement.

The Chicago Underflow Plan, also known as the Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP), is a major infrastructure project aimed at addressing urban flooding and water pollution in the Greater Chicago area. The plan involves upgrading the combined sewer system, constructing tunnels and reservoirs, and enhancing treatment plants to manage stormwater and sewage more effectively. The project is expected to reduce basement flooding, improve water quality, and increase water supply reliability. While it promises economic and social benefits, concerns have been raised about environmental impacts, particularly during construction, and the long-term effects on local ecosystems.

The plan involves upgrading the combined sewer system, constructing tunnels and reservoirs, and enhancing treatment plants to manage stormwater and sewage more effectively. The project is expected to reduce basement flooding, improve water quality, and increase water supply reliability. Greater Chicago area, Cook County, Illinois
Metropolitan Sanitary District of Greater Chicago (MSDGC)

The Chicago Underflow Plan was proposed in response to chronic flooding and water pollution issues in the Greater Chicago area, exacerbated by heavy rainfall and an outdated combined sewer system. While the project aims to significantly improve urban water management and reduce flooding, it has faced public concerns regarding potential environmental impacts during construction and the effects on local ecosystems. Despite these concerns, the project has moved forward with the goal of enhancing water quality and reliability for the region.
